narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 70% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which is above the typical 3.21 average, and an impressive LSAT score of 39, higher than the usual 36.70—those are strengths that stand out compared to most applicants. However, your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the bottom of the scale (far below the average around the middle), were the biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure. Your Year 3 decile had the strongest influence in this direction, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, meaning your early academic struggles signaled higher risk to the model more than anything else. Even your LSAT score, despite being strong, contributed slightly toward the failure prediction in your specific case, ranking third in influence, with family income in the upper-middle bracket coming next. Your background as a white male planning full-time studies aligned with common profiles but nudged the odds a bit further toward risk, though with less impact than your grades. Overall, your low early deciles set you apart from typical students who land in mid-range positions, overshadowing your later achievements like UGPA and LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how these patterns played out for you. To boost your future chances, focus on rebuilding your academic foundation through targeted study habits or supplemental courses, as improving performance indicators like those early deciles is key to shifting predictions. You might also consider LSAT prep or GPA-boosting grad work if possible, since stronger scores there could counterbalance past lows. Many students turn things around with persistence, and revisiting your application after such changes could make a big difference. Hang in there—you've already shown capability in areas that matter.
